breaking_news.text: Fed holds rates steady for third consecutive meeting — Chair signals data-dependent approach through Q2 2026

market_stories.title: Fed Holds Rates — Third Meeting in a Row
market_stories.summary: The Federal Reserve kept interest rates at 4.25–4.5% for the third consecutive meeting. Chair Powell said the committee needs more confidence that inflation is falling before cutting. For students: higher rates mean your savings account pays more, but loans cost more too. If you plan to take out student loans, this matters directly.

market_stories.title: Why Gold Just Hit an All-Time High
market_stories.summary: Gold crossed $3,090 per ounce — a record. When investors get nervous about the economy, they buy gold as a 'safe haven' asset. That surge tells you a lot about current market anxiety and uncertainty.

market_stories.title: VIX Drops 4% — What the 'Fear Index' Is Telling Us
market_stories.summary: The VIX measures how much volatility traders expect in the market. When it falls, it means traders feel calmer. This week's drop suggests investors are less panicked than last week, even with rate uncertainty in the air.

world_stories.title: New Tariffs on Steel: What It Means for Prices
world_stories.summary: The US announced new 25% tariffs on imported steel. Tariffs are taxes on foreign goods — they protect US companies but raise prices for consumers. Think higher car and appliance costs in 6–12 months.

world_stories.title: China's Economy Is Slowing — Should You Care?
world_stories.summary: China's GDP growth fell to 4.5%, below the 5% target. Since China makes a massive share of global goods, a slowdown there ripples into supply chains, tech hardware, and commodity prices worldwide.

world_stories.title: US Unemployment Holds at 4.1% — Is That Good?
world_stories.summary: The job market remains resilient despite high rates. Full employment is typically considered around 4–5%. The key question: if jobs stay strong, will the Fed feel pressure to cut rates at all?

personal_stories.title: How to Open Your First Brokerage Account at 17
personal_stories.summary: You can start investing before 18 with a custodial account — a brokerage account a parent opens on your behalf. Fidelity and Charles Schwab both offer these for free with no minimums. Here's exactly how to do it in under 20 minutes.

personal_stories.title: The Roth IRA: The Best Tax Hack Teenagers Have Access To
personal_stories.summary: If you have a part-time job, you can contribute to a Roth IRA — money grows completely tax-free for life. Starting at 16 vs 25 can mean $200,000+ more at retirement. This is the single best financial move a teenager can make right now.

tech_stories.title: Nvidia's AI Chips Are Behind Everything Right Now
tech_stories.summary: From ChatGPT to self-driving cars, Nvidia's GPUs power most modern AI. The company's market cap just crossed $2 trillion again — meaning investors bet AI is here to stay. Understanding why helps you understand the whole tech market.

tech_stories.title: Apple's AI Strategy Is Finally Taking Shape
tech_stories.summary: Apple Intelligence rolled out globally this quarter, integrating AI into every iPhone. Unlike Nvidia which sells the 'picks and shovels' of AI, Apple bets on AI as a reason to buy new hardware. Two very different business models worth studying.

tech_stories.title: What Is a Valuation — And Why Does Every Startup Claim Billions?
tech_stories.summary: When a startup says it's 'valued at $10 billion,' that number comes from the last funding round, not actual revenue. Valuation is what investors agree a company is worth — and it can be wildly wrong in both directions.

word_of_day.definition: A graph showing interest rates on US government bonds at different maturity lengths. When short-term rates are higher than long-term ones (inverted), it has historically predicted recessions.
word_of_day.example: Analysts pointed to the inverted yield curve as a warning sign, noting that short-term Treasury bills were paying more than 10-year bonds — a pattern that preceded each of the last seven US recessions.

quiz.question: If the Federal Reserve raises interest rates, what typically happens to the stock market?

options.text: A. Stocks usually go up immediately
B. Stocks often fall because borrowing becomes more expensive
C. Nothing — rates and stocks are unrelated
D. The stock market closes for the day

quiz.explanation: When the Fed raises rates, companies pay more to borrow money, reducing profits. Investors also earn more from 'safe' bonds, making risky stocks less attractive. Both effects push prices down.

explainer_strip.def: The S&P 500 is a basket of 500 large US companies. When it rises, it means most big businesses are doing well.
Interest rates affect everything — your future mortgage, student loans, and how much banks pay you to save money.
US inflation sits at 2.8% — meaning something that cost $100 last year now costs $102.80. The Fed's goal is 2%.
If you invest $100/month starting at 17 at a 7% return, you will have ~$525,000 by age 65. That's compound interest.
